title: Getting Started with bsvarPost
text: Historical decomposition split two complementary workflows: full-sample contribution paths tidy_hd(), plot_hd_stacked(), plot_hd_overlay(), plot_hd_total(), plot_hd_lines(); event-window summaries tidy_hd_event(), plot_hd_event(), plot_hd_event_share(), plot_hd_event_cumulative(), plot_hd_event_distribution(). Use plot_hd_overlay() first compare main contribution paths within one variable time:  use plot_hd_stacked() composition view. stacks structural shock contributions time, include_baseline = TRUE available want explicit non-shock reconstruction term well.  plot_hd_total() checks decomposition observed path, plot_hd_lines() provides detailed component--component inspection view combined plots compressed. full-sample plots identified period worth summarizing, move event-window helpers. tidy_hd_event(), plot_hd_event(), plot_hd_event_share(), plot_hd_event_cumulative(), plot_hd_event_distribution(), shock_ranking() summarize rank HD information selected window.
code: hd_tbl <- tidy_hd(post) plot_hd_overlay(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3) plot_hd_stacked(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3) plot_hd_total(post, variables = "gdp", shocks = c("gs", "ttr")) plot_hd_lines(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3)  hd_times <- unique(as.character(tidy_hd(post, draws = TRUE)$time)) plot_hd_event(post, start = hd_times[1], end = hd_times[2]) plot_hd_event_share(post, start = hd_times[1], end = hd_times[2], top_n = 3) plot_hd_event_cumulative(post, start = hd_times[1], end = hd_times[2], top_n = 3) plot_hd_event_distribution(post, start = hd_times[1], end = hd_times[2], top_n = 3) shock_ranking(post, start = hd_times[1], end = hd_times[2], ranking = "absolute")

text: Post-Estimation Workflows vignette — covers historical decomposition, including full-sample HD plots event-window composition views, plus hypothesis testing, peak/duration response summaries, representative-draw selection. Hypothesis Testing vignette — formal posterior probability statements via hypothesis_irf(), joint_hypothesis_irf(), simultaneous_irf().

title: Hypothesis Testing in bsvarPost
text: direct question: fraction posterior draws satisfy condition given horizon? posterior_prob column reports fraction draws satisfying condition — direct probability statement, p-value. test applies cumulative dynamic multipliers (CDMs), standard measure fiscal multiplier effects: functions return bsvar_post_tbl posterior probability, mean median gap threshold, equal-tailed credible bounds.
code: # Posterior probability that gdp responds positively to gs at horizon 8 h_irf <- hypothesis_irf(   post,   variables = 3,   shocks    = 2,   horizon   = 8,   relation  = ">",   value     = 0 ) h_irf #> # A tibble: 1 × 16 #>   model  object_type variable shock horizon relation posterior_prob mean_gap #>   <chr>  <chr>       <chr>    <chr>   <dbl> <chr>             <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 model1 irf         gdp      gs          8 >                  0.11 -0.00186 #> # ℹ 8 more variables: median_gap <dbl>, lower_gap <dbl>, upper_gap <dbl>, #> #   rhs_variable <chr>, rhs_shock <chr>, rhs_horizon <dbl>, rhs_value <dbl>, #> #   absolute <lgl> # Same test for the cumulative dynamic multiplier at horizon 8 h_cdm <- hypothesis_cdm(   post,   variables = 3,   shocks    = 2,   horizon   = 8,   relation  = ">",   value     = 0 ) h_cdm #> # A tibble: 1 × 16 #>   model  object_type variable shock horizon relation posterior_prob mean_gap #>   <chr>  <chr>       <chr>    <chr>   <dbl> <chr>             <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 model1 cdm         gdp      gs          8 >                 0.185 -0.00840 #> # ℹ 8 more variables: median_gap <dbl>, lower_gap <dbl>, upper_gap <dbl>, #> #   rhs_variable <chr>, rhs_shock <chr>, rhs_horizon <dbl>, rhs_value <dbl>, #> #   absolute <lgl>

title: Hypothesis Testing in bsvarPost
text: stronger question whether GDP positive every horizon impact horizon 8. joint probability typically much lower single pointwise probability. n_constraints records many conditions intersected. pre-rendered figure showing probability profile across horizons:  figure illustrates pointwise probabilities can remain high joint probability substantially lower — draw fails even one horizon excluded joint count.
code: # Joint probability: gdp positive at ALL horizons 0 through 8 jh <- joint_hypothesis_irf(   post,   variable = "gdp",   shock    = "gs",   horizon  = 0:8,   relation = ">",   value    = 0 ) jh #> # A tibble: 1 × 13 #>   model object_type relation posterior_prob n_constraints variable shock horizon #>   <chr> <chr>       <chr>             <dbl>         <int> <chr>    <chr> <chr>   #> 1 mode… joint_irf   >                 0.095             9 gdp      gs    0,1,2,… #> # ℹ 5 more variables: rhs_variable <chr>, rhs_shock <chr>, rhs_horizon <dbl>, #> #   rhs_value <dbl>, absolute <lgl>

title: Hypothesis Testing in bsvarPost
text: Pointwise credible intervals cover horizon independently. Simultaneous bands wider provide stronger guarantee: 90% posterior probability, entire IRF path lies inside band. critical_value column records sup-norm threshold: draws whose maximum deviation posterior median exceeds value excluded. approach applies CDMs: CDM band covers entire cumulative multiplier path impact horizon 20 90% posterior probability.
code: # 90% simultaneous band for the gdp response to the gs shock sim_irf <- simultaneous_irf(   post,   horizon   = 20,   variables = 3,   shocks    = 2 ) head(sim_irf) #> # A tibble: 6 × 10 #>   model  object_type      variable shock horizon     median    lower   upper #>   <chr>  <chr>            <chr>    <chr>   <dbl>      <dbl>    <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 model1 simultaneous_irf gdp      gs          0 -0.0000935 -0.00317 0.00299 #> 2 model1 simultaneous_irf gdp      gs          1 -0.000250  -0.00333 0.00283 #> 3 model1 simultaneous_irf gdp      gs          2 -0.000424  -0.00350 0.00266 #> 4 model1 simultaneous_irf gdp      gs          3 -0.000557  -0.00364 0.00252 #> 5 model1 simultaneous_irf gdp      gs          4 -0.000702  -0.00378 0.00238 #> 6 model1 simultaneous_irf gdp      gs          5 -0.000839  -0.00392 0.00224 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: simultaneous_prob <dbl>, critical_value <dbl> # 90% simultaneous band for the cumulative fiscal multiplier sim_cdm <- simultaneous_cdm(   post,   horizon   = 20,   variables = 3,   shocks    = 2 ) head(sim_cdm) #> # A tibble: 6 × 10 #>   model  object_type      variable shock horizon     median   lower  upper #>   <chr>  <chr>            <chr>    <chr>   <dbl>      <dbl>   <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1 model1 simultaneous_cdm gdp      gs          0 -0.0000935 -0.0384 0.0382 #> 2 model1 simultaneous_cdm gdp      gs          1 -0.000302  -0.0386 0.0380 #> 3 model1 simultaneous_cdm gdp      gs          2 -0.000748  -0.0391 0.0376 #> 4 model1 simultaneous_cdm gdp      gs          3 -0.00134   -0.0397 0.0370 #> 5 model1 simultaneous_cdm gdp      gs          4 -0.00211   -0.0404 0.0362 #> 6 model1 simultaneous_cdm gdp      gs          5 -0.00295   -0.0413 0.0354 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: simultaneous_prob <dbl>, critical_value <dbl>

title: Hypothesis Testing in bsvarPost
text: Beyond sign, researchers often care magnitude. fraction draws show fiscal multiplier exceeding 1.0 horizon 8? magnitude_audit() reports often posterior satisfies magnitude condition — constrain model. Comparing across lag lengths: higher posterior_prob alternative 3-lag specification indicates support large multipliers richer lag dynamics allowed.

title: Hypothesis Testing in bsvarPost
text: bsvarPost provides coherent hypothesis testing toolkit Bayesian SVAR analysis: hypothesis_irf() / hypothesis_cdm(): pointwise posterior probabilities specified horizons joint_hypothesis_irf(): joint probability across selected horizons simultaneous_irf() / simultaneous_cdm(): bands covering full path magnitude_audit(): often magnitude condition satisfied results proper Bayesian posterior probability statements, frequentist p-values: probability 0.92 means 92% posterior draws satisfy condition, asymptotic approximation required.

title: Post-Estimation Workflows in bsvarPost
text: Pointwise posterior medians may correspond one coherent structural draw. median_target_irf() finds stored draw whose IRF matrix closest (L2 distance) posterior median. result single self-consistent model typifies posterior center. draw_index slot records posterior draw selected. can used extract quantities (CDMs, FEVD) draw fully coherent representative model. pre-rendered figure posterior full S = 200 resolution:  representative IRF gs spending shock shows typical fiscal transmission shape: immediate positive effect government spending , gdp response peaking quarters later.

title: Post-Estimation Workflows in bsvarPost
text: Researchers often need report precisely effects arrive long persist. bsvarPost provides four timing summaries, full posterior uncertainty. Peak response — horizon fiscal spending shock produce largest gdp effect? Duration response — many quarters cumulative multiplier remain positive? Half-life — quickly impulse response decay peak? Time threshold — cumulative multiplier first exceed zero? four summaries characterise fiscal spending effect arrives, large becomes, quickly dissipates — standard set timing statistics fiscal multiplier papers.
code: peak_response(post, type = "irf", horizon = 20, variables = 3, shocks = 2) #> # A tibble: 1 × 14 #>   model  object_type variable shock mean_value median_value sd_value lower_value #>   <chr>  <chr>       <chr>    <chr>      <dbl>        <dbl>    <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 model1 peak_irf    gdp      gs        0.0379   -0.0000250    0.537    -0.00114 #> # code: cmp_peak <- compare_peak_response(   baseline    = post,   alternative = post_alt,   type        = "irf",   horizon     = 20,   variables   = 3,   shocks      = 2 ) cmp_peak #> # A tibble: 2 × 14 #>   model  object_type variable shock mean_value median_value sd_value lower_value #>   <chr>  <chr>       <chr>    <chr>      <dbl>        <dbl>    <dbl>       <dbl> #> 1 basel… peak_irf    gdp      gs        0.0379   -0.0000250  5.37e-1   -0.00114  #> 2 alter… peak_irf    gdp      gs    66131.        0.000115   9.35e+5   -0.000904 #> # ℹ 6 more variables: upper_value <dbl>, mean_horizon <dbl>, #> #   median_horizon <dbl>, sd_horizon <dbl>, lower_horizon <dbl>, #> #   upper_horizon <dbl> compare_duration_response(   baseline    = post,   alternative = post_alt,   type        = "cdm",   horizon     = 20,   variables   = 3,   shocks      = 2,   relation    = ">",   value       = 0 ) #> # A tibble: 2 × 12 #>   model       object_type  variable shock relation threshold mode  mean_duration #>   <chr>       <chr>        <chr>    <chr> <chr>        <dbl> <chr>         <dbl> #> 1 baseline    duration_cdm gdp      gs    >                0 cons…          3.95 #> 2 alternative duration_cdm gdp      gs    >                0 cons…          4.16 #> # ℹ 4 more variables: median_duration <dbl>, sd_duration <dbl>, #> #   lower_duration <dbl>, upper_duration <dbl> as_kable(cmp_peak, preset = "compact")

title: Post-Estimation Workflows in bsvarPost
text: Historical decompositions (HD) answer two complementary questions: structural shock contributions evolve full sample, contributions aggregate selected event window? Start full-sample HD object. tidy_hd() returns contribution structural shock variable point time, feeds dedicated plotting helpers. Use plot_hd_overlay() first want compare main shock contributions move time within one variable. best first-look diagnostic keeps contribution paths common panel without mixing raw observed level. pre-rendered full-sample HD overlay plot S = 200 posterior:  plot_hd_stacked() gives composition view time. default shows stacked structural shock contributions; add include_baseline = TRUE want explicit non-shock reconstruction term well.  plot_hd_total() plays different role: compares observed series reconstructed decomposition total, natural validation view main shock drivers clear. overlay stacked views become compressed, plot_hd_lines() gives detailed inspection view separating component paths rather forcing one panel. full-sample HD plots identified period interest, move event-window summaries. tidy_hd_event() aggregates contributions chosen window. us_fiscal_lsuw sample begins 1948. examine shocks drove fiscal dynamics first year (four quarters: 1948.25 1948.75): plot_hd_event() keeps window summary contribution units, plot_hd_event_share() converts event within-window shares. plot_hd_event_cumulative() shows contributions accumulate inside window, plot_hd_event_distribution() focuses posterior uncertainty shock. shock_ranking() event-summary ranking tool: orders structural shocks absolute contribution target variable selected window. shock moved gdp ? composition-oriented event view, plot_hd_event_share() rescales event-window contributions shares: pre-rendered event-share composition plot:  pre-rendered HD event figure richer S = 200 run:
code: hd_full <- tidy_hd(post) plot_hd_overlay(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3) plot_hd_stacked(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3) plot_hd_total(post, variables = "gdp", shocks = c("gs", "ttr")) plot_hd_lines(post, variables = "gdp", top_n = 3) hd_event <- tidy_hd_event(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75") head(hd_event) #> # A tibble: 6 × 11 #>   model  object_type variable shock event_start event_end     mean    median #>   <chr>  <chr>       <chr>    <chr> <chr>       <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 model1 hd_event    gdp      gdp   1948.25     1948.75    3.28     3.32     #> 2 model1 hd_event    gs       gdp   1948.25     1948.75    0.0116   0.000610 #> 3 model1 hd_event    ttr      gdp   1948.25     1948.75   -0.282   -0.289    #> 4 model1 hd_event    gdp      gs    1948.25     1948.75   -0.0295  -0.0188   #> 5 model1 hd_event    gs       gs    1948.25     1948.75   -0.906   -0.880    #> 6 model1 hd_event    ttr      gs    1948.25     1948.75    0.00140  0.00680  #> # ℹ 3 more variables: sd <dbl>, lower <dbl>, upper <dbl> shock_ranking(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75", ranking = "absolute") #> # A tibble: 9 × 14 #>   model  object_type variable shock event_start event_end     mean    median #>   <chr>  <chr>       <chr>    <chr> <chr>       <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 model1 hd_event    gdp      gdp   1948.25     1948.75    3.28     3.32     #> 2 model1 hd_event    gdp      ttr   1948.25     1948.75    0.520    0.458    #> 3 model1 hd_event    gdp      gs    1948.25     1948.75   -0.0295  -0.0188   #> 4 model1 hd_event    gs       gs    1948.25     1948.75   -0.906   -0.880    #> 5 model1 hd_event    gs       ttr   1948.25     1948.75    0.0646   0.00766  #> 6 model1 hd_event    gs       gdp   1948.25     1948.75    0.0116   0.000610 #> 7 model1 hd_event    ttr      ttr   1948.25     1948.75   -7.94    -8.01     #> 8 model1 hd_event    ttr      gdp   1948.25     1948.75   -0.282   -0.289    #> 9 model1 hd_event    ttr      gs    1948.25     1948.75    0.00140  0.00680  #> # ℹ 6 more variables: sd <dbl>, lower <dbl>, upper <dbl>, ranking <chr>, #> #   rank_score <dbl>, rank <int> plot_hd_event(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75") plot_hd_event_share(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75", top_n = 3) plot_hd_event_cumulative(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75", top_n = 3) plot_hd_event_distribution(post, start = "1948.25", end = "1948.75", top_n = 3)

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: Initial CRAN release.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: variable shock (singular) arguments deprecated favour variables shocks (plural) throughout package. singular forms still work produce warning; removed future version.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: tidy_irf() extracts impulse responses PosteriorBSVAR, PosteriorBSVARSV, PosteriorBSVARMIX, PosteriorBSVARMSH, PosteriorBSVART, PosteriorBSVARSIGN objects tidy tibbles credible-interval columns. tidy_cdm() extracts cumulative dynamic multipliers supported posterior types. tidy_fevd() extracts forecast error variance decompositions (reported 0–100 percentage scale used ‘bsvars’). tidy_shocks() extracts structural shocks. tidy_hd() extracts historical decompositions. tidy_forecast() extracts --sample forecasts. cdm() estimates cumulative dynamic multipliers directly supported posterior object, bridging ‘bsvars’ ‘bsvarSIGNs’ single call.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: hypothesis_irf() hypothesis_cdm() compute posterior probabilities user-specified inequality constraints impulse responses cumulative dynamic multipliers. joint_hypothesis_irf() joint_hypothesis_cdm() extend hypothesis testing joint constraints across multiple variable–shock–horizon combinations. simultaneous_irf() simultaneous_cdm() compute simultaneous credible bands exact joint coverage.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: representative_irf() representative_cdm() identify posterior draw closest pointwise median across impulse-response CDM paths. most_likely_admissible_irf() most_likely_admissible_cdm() select draw highest kernel score among sign-restriction-admissible draws.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: peak_response() locates horizon maximum absolute impulse response posterior distribution. duration_response() summarises number horizons response remains threshold. half_life_response() computes posterior distribution half-life response decay. time_to_threshold() computes posterior distribution first horizon response crosses user-specified threshold.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: compare_irf(), compare_cdm(), compare_fevd() compare tidy extractions across two models side--side. compare_peak_response(), compare_duration_response(), compare_half_life_response(), compare_time_to_threshold() compare response-shape summaries across models. compare_restrictions() compares restriction-audit results across models. compare_hd_event() compares event-window historical decomposition summaries across models. compare_acceptance_diagnostics() compares sign-restriction acceptance diagnostics across models.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: restriction_audit() evaluates whether posterior draws satisfy set structural sign narrative restrictions. magnitude_audit() checks whether impulse responses satisfy magnitude-based restrictions explicit variable shock arguments. acceptance_diagnostics() reports draw-level admissibility statistics bsvarSIGNs posteriors, summary() print() methods.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: tidy_hd_event() summarises historical decomposition contributions within user-specified event window. shock_ranking() ranks shocks contribution target variable within event window.

title: bsvarPost 1.0.0
text: autoplot() support tidy extractors (PosteriorIR, PosteriorCDM, PosteriorFEVD, PosteriorSHOCKS, PosteriorHD, PosteriorFORECAST), comparison outputs, simultaneous bands, joint hypothesis objects. theme_bsvarpost() provides minimal publication-ready ggplot2 theme. style_bsvar_plot() applies consistent axis, legend, colour styling bsvarPost ggplot2 output. publish_bsvar_plot() applies family-aware publication templates across comparison, representative, diagnostics, event-study, joint-inference outputs. as_gt(), as_flextable(), as_kable() convert tidy bsvarPost tables formatted gt, flextable, knitr::kable outputs publication documents. write_bsvar_csv() writes tidy bsvarPost table CSV. report_bundle() produces paired plot--table bundle suitable publication workflows, support representative-response, acceptance-diagnostics, simultaneous-band, joint-hypothesis, event-study objects. report_table() generates compact default publication-oriented table layouts family-specific labels subtitles.
